title: Cautious Risk Takers: Investor Preferences and Demand for Active Management
abstract: Despite their mediocre mean performance, actively managed mutual funds are distinct from passive funds in their return distributions. Active value funds better hedge downside risk, while active growth funds better capture upside potential. Since such performance features may appeal to investors with tail-overweighting preferences, we show that preferences for downside protection and upside potential estimated from the empirical pricing kernel can help explain active fund flows in the value and growth categories, respectively. This effect of investor risk preferences varies significantly with funds' downside-hedging and upside-capturing ability, with levels of active management, and across retirement and retail funds.

article.introduction:
Despite the mediocre performanceof actively managed mutual funds relative to their passively managed counterparts, assets under active management continue to significantly outweigh those of index funds.1This puzzle has attracted considerable interest in the mutual fund literature. While some studies attempt to rationalize investments in actively managed funds by modeling state-dependent managerial effort or skill, in this paper we explore components in the demand for active management that stem from investor preferences for upside potential or downside risk protection. We show that, compared with passive benchmarks, active value funds achieve greater downside hedging while active growth funds deliver stronger upside potential. We then link variation in investor preferences to fund flows and show that proxies for investor preferences have significant explanatory power for flows into actively managed growth and value funds. These findings contribute to our understanding of investor demand for actively managed mutual funds.
We begin by constructing bootstrapped samples of monthly returns of actively managed mutual funds and their passive benchmarks to compare their return distributions.2To isolate the active component of fund returns, we construct the market-neutral excess returns of active funds over passive benchmarks. We use the skewness of returns and excess return (idiosyncratic) skewness to measure the upside potential of active funds. To measure downside protection of active portfolios, we use co-skewness with market returns, downmarket betas of excess returns, and excess returns' loadings on aggregate jump and volatility risk factors constructed from straddle portfolios of S&P 500 index options (Cremers, Halling, and Weinbaum (2015), CHW henceforth).3
The results of our analysis of active fund returns and their excess returns over passive benchmarks suggest that the return distributions of active funds are quite different from those of their passive benchmarks in terms of their downside-hedging and upside-capturing properties. While both active growth and value funds possess some of these return features, their extent varies considerably. On balance, active value funds provide better downside-hedging ability. Relative to passive benchmarks, active value funds have lower co-skewness with the market, have lower downside beta, and provide significant hedging against aggregate jump risk. On the other hand, active growth funds exhibit more pronounced upside-capturing features. Relative to passive benchmarks, they have considerably higher skewness and positive idiosyncratic skewness. In addition, while their overall market beta tends to be high, active growth funds also reduce beta during market downturns. However, unlike active value funds, active growth funds do not provide hedging against jump risk.
In addition to using the market portfolio as the simplest benchmark, we employ two other benchmarking methods. The first benchmark is constructed based on holdings-matched portfolios. Relative to return-based benchmarks, holdings-based benchmarks have the advantage of abstracting from factors affecting fund returns that are unrelated to active equity investment strategies (e.g., fees and cash reserve). In addition, they allow us to control for differences in diversification (including number of different stocks in the portfolio) and security characteristics between active and passive funds (Bollen and Busse (2001), Huang, Sialm, and Zhang (2011)). To construct this benchmark, every quarter for each active fund in our sample, we replace each of its holdings with a randomly drawn stock with the same size, book-to-market, and momentum (SBM) quintile ranks. The returns of this hypothetical portfolio are used as our passive benchmark. To construct the other benchmark, we follow Berk and van Binsbergen (2015) and use four-factor model (Fama-French three factors plus momentum) predicted returns. Regardless of the benchmark used, our results point to consistent and marked differences in the return distribution across active and passive funds and across different investment objectives. The distinct properties of active fund returns cannot be explained by the potentially different characteristics of securities held by active versus passive funds, nor can they be attributed to lower diversification of active funds' portfolios compared to those of passive funds.
Given the observed differences in the returns of active versus passive funds, we hypothesize that active funds may appeal to investors who value upside potential and downside protection. While we refer to investors with such preferences by an oxymoron (“cautious risk takers”) in the title, these preferences are widely supported by experimental evidence (Camerer and Ho (1994), Camerer (1995), Starmer (2000)). Several theoretical models of portfolio choice demonstrate that investors who overweight tail probabilities of return distributions may prefer portfolio returns with limited downside risk and high upside potential.4Changes in investor expectation, risk attitude, or investor composition would naturally generate flows in and out of different styles of funds. Based on the distributional properties of active funds' returns, we hypothesize that flows into actively managed value funds increase with investor aversion to downside risk while flows into actively managed growth funds increase with investor preference for upside potential.
To explore whether this channel of demand for active funds is theoretically plausible, we consider a portfolio choice problem of an investor who has access to a risk-free asset and to active and passive funds with a joint return distribution as bootstrapped from the data. We assume that the investor maximizes rank-dependent expected utility (RDEU; Quiggin (1982), Yaari (1987)) with a Prelec (1998) inverse-S probability weighting function. This weighting function has two parameters, which allows for a variety of risk attitudes toward tail events, including joint preferences for upside potential and downside protection.
We solve numerically for the optimal portfolios and examine the comparative statics of the demand for active management with respect to the parameters of the probability weighting function. Our results demonstrate that the observed differences in return distributions between active and passive funds are quantitatively relevant to an RDEU investor. In particular, we show that demand for active value funds increases when the investor has greater preference for downside protection, while demand for active growth funds increases when the investor exhibits greater upside-seeking preference. We also use the model to compute the implied value of active funds and find that an investor would be willing to pay reasonable fees for access to the active fund portfolios.
To construct empirical measures of investor preference for upside potential and downside protection, we use risk-neutral distributions of returns estimated from the S&P 500 index option prices and physical distributions estimated from the underlying index returns. The pricing kernel (PK) contains information about investor risk attitude and thus can be used to construct empirical measures of risk preferences in different parts of the return distribution. Following Polkovnichenko and Zhao (2013), we construct two sets of proxies at the monthly frequency: (1) α and β from the Prelec (1998) two-parameter probability weighting function fitted to the PK, and (2) the left-tail and right-tail slopes of the PK. We define PK downside and upside slopes, respectively, as the ratio of risk-neutral to physical cumulative distribution function (CDF) on the left side and the ratio of risk-neutral to physical de-cumulative probabilities on the right side of the return distribution.
We use these two alternative measures to demonstrate the robustness of our results to the proxies used for investor preferences. The estimates of the Prelec structural parameters are directly associated with preferences for downside protection and upside potential. They use information in the entire PK and, unlike the PK slopes, do not require assumptions on which range of moneyness is used to compute risk preferences. However, they do require assumptions on the functional form of the probability weighting function. The PK slopes do not impose additional assumptions on preferences beyond those needed for the estimation of the PK. Although they do not correspond to a specific structural measure of investor risk attitude, they do have generally intuitive economic interpretations independent of the utility model. Risk-neutral probability is proportional to the “state price” investors are willing to pay to receive a unit of payoff in a particular state. This price could be higher because investors put more value on the state or because the state is more likely to be realized. To isolate the investor preference component, we normalize the cumulative risk-neutral probability by the cumulative physical probability. Thus, the ratio (slope) can be interpreted as the average price of risk over a range of states: it is higher when investors' willingness to pay per unit of event probability is higher. A larger value of the left slope indicates stronger aversion to downside risk, while a larger value of the right slope indicates a stronger preference for upside potential.
We use S&P 500 index option prices to estimate the PK, construct the PK slopes, and fit the Prelec functions.5Estimating the empirical PK and investor preferences from the option market has been a commonly applied approach (see, e.g., Aït-Sahalia and Lo (2000), Jackwerth (2000)). The no-arbitrage link between stock and option markets ensures that the empirical PK reflects the risk preferences of stock investors even if not all of them trade index options. In addition, since investments by U.S. open-end equity mutual funds account for a significant part of stock market capitalization, our index option-based risk preference estimates should be representative of the risk attitude of average mutual fund investors, even though many of them might not trade options at all.6
We next empirically examine the impact of investor preferences on the demand of active funds. Using flows into U.S. open-end mutual funds during 1996 to 2008 period, we show that proxies for investor preferences have significant explanatory power for monthly fund flows into actively managed funds. The results hold regardless of which type of preference measure we employ. The economic magnitude of the effects is comparable to that of past performance—one of the most significant determinants of fund flows. Consistent with our hypotheses, we find that flows into actively managed value funds increase significantly with investor aversion to downside risk while flows into actively managed growth funds increase significantly with investor preferences for upside potential. These findings suggest that active value funds appeal to investors when they seek downside risk protection while active growth funds appeal to investors when they seek upside potential. Note that these two types of behaviors may not be mutually exclusive and our results need not imply investor segmentation in the mutual fund market.7
Our results are not a simple reflection of investor sentiment. While investor sentiment may lead to strong demand for either downside protection or upside potential, our framework allows for the coexistence of demand for both downside protection and upside seeking and can differentiate individual demand for value versus growth funds, that is, the demand for investments with payoffs in specific parts of the fund return distribution. Therefore, it is not clear whether investor sentiment can serve as a substitute for our measures. Indeed, we find low correlations between the preference proxies we use and commonly used sentiment measures during our sample period. We include the Baker and Wurgler (2006,2007) sentiment measure as a control in all of our specifications and additionally show that the effect of investor risk preferences on fund flows continues to hold after controlling for a National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER) recession indicator and the market volatility index (VIX).
To further establish the link between investor preferences and the observed pattern of fund flows, we present several cross-sectional analyses that help pin down the role of investor preferences for downside protection and upside potential. We first compare the flow sensitivity to risk preferences across funds with different downside protection and upside-capturing properties. To separate funds with different downside protection ability, we sort funds based on their downmarket betas (i.e., beta conditional on low overall market returns) and, in a separate exercise, on their loadings on the aggregate jump risk factor of CHW. Funds that have lower downside betas or higher loadings on the jump risk factor are expected to provide better downside protection for investors. Consistent with these conjectures, we find a stronger sensitivity of flows to the preference measures for downside protection among value funds with lower downside betas and higher loadings on the jump risk factor. To examine cross-sectional variation in the effect of upside potential preferences on flows, we group funds based on the skewness of their recent returns. We find that for growth funds with higher return skewness, flows are more sensitive to proxies for the upside-seeking preference.
Abstracting from specific measures of downside protection and upside seeking in fund performance, we also group funds based on the extent of their active management as proxied by the Active Share measure of Cremers and Petajisto (2009). To the extent that low Active Share funds resemble passive funds and thus provide little downside protection or upside potential, we expect to see weaker effects of investor risk preferences on flows into low Active Share funds, as compared to high Active Share funds. This is indeed what we find: for funds with higher Active Share, flows into large value (LV) funds are significantly more sensitive to measures of downside protection preferences, and flows into active large growth (LG) funds are significantly more sensitive to measures of upside-seeking preferences.
Finally, we examine flows into retirement versus retail funds, which may serve to accommodate investors' desired exposures to downside protection versus upside potential, respectively. Specifically, we expect investors to treat their retirement savings more conservatively than other investments and therefore flows into retirement funds should have lower loadings on the upside-seeking preference yet higher loadings on the downside protection preference than flows into retail funds. Our results indicate that flows into retirement funds in the value category exhibit a stronger sensitivity to the preference for downside protection than nonretirement retail funds with the same style. Also, flows into nonretirement retail funds in the growth category demonstrate larger exposures to the upside-seeking preference compared to retirement investments in growth funds.
Our paper is related to the recent literature studying flows into actively managed funds. Glode (2011) and Kacperczyk, van Nieuwerburgh, and Veldkamp (2016), for example, consider models with endogenous efforts of mutual fund managers that vary with economic conditions, while Savov (2014) models active funds as providing hedging to investors with nontraded income exposures. The models in these studies generate time-varying active fund performance, which is attractive to investors because funds perform better in market downturns.8Our paper complements this literature by focusing on distinct investor preferences for the distributional properties of active fund returns and by linking such preferences to investor demand for actively managed funds.
Moreover, we separately consider investor preferences for upside seeking and downside protection and analyze their respective relationships with flows into growth versus value funds. Therefore, while prior studies analyze investor demand for actively managed funds as a whole, our paper provides cross-sectional implications for fund flows into active funds with different performance features and investment styles. We thus provide insights into the growing popularity of style investments among mutual fund investors. Furthermore, we conduct several unique cross-sectional analyses using fund or investor characteristics to help establish the link between investor risk attitude and the demand for actively managed mutual funds. The findings from these analyses indicate that fund and investor characteristics jointly determine investors' asset allocation decisions beyond prior documented channels such as marketing or behavioral biases. Our paper thus identifies investor preferences for upside potential and downside protection as important determinants of the demand for active management and provides a new perspective on what active funds may offer investors, beyond their mean performance.
Our paper also speaks to the current debate in the literature about measures of managerial skill and the rationality of fund flows. Berk and Green (2004) and Berk and van Binsbergen (2015) argue that managerial skill is rationally reflected in fund size, and by extension in fund flows. Our work identifies new mechanisms of fund flows that are related to investor preferences for fund performance in the tails of the return distribution. This suggests a new dimension of managerial skill related to the tail risk properties of active fund returns. Moreover, we show that the features of investor preferences that drive fund flows are consistent with nonexpected utility (non-EU) models with independent mechanisms emphasizing higher order moments of fund performance. Such deviations from the standard utility models appear to suggest a promising research avenue for identifying relevant factors that drive investor preferences and affect asset prices.
The rest of the paper is organized as follows. In SectionI, we describe our data and discuss summary statistics for our sample funds. SectionIIcompares the return distributions of active versus passive funds. SectionIIIintroduces a model of portfolio choice with tail overweighting preferences and discusses the construction of proxies for upside and downside preferences that are based on index option prices. SectionIVprovides results of empirical analyses on the relation between fund flows and investor risk preferences. SectionVpresents results of cross-sectional analyses of this relation. SectionVIconcludes the paper. The Internet Appendix contains methodological details on the estimation of investor preferences from option prices and supplementary robustness checks.
article.I. Data:
Our empirical analyses mainly use two types of data: S&P 500 index option prices and mutual fund flows and returns, both at the individual fund level and at the investment category level. We obtain data on S&P 500 index options (symbol SPX) from OptionMetrics for the period February 1996 to December 2008. This period is also our main sample period for analyses involving fund flows because they rely on risk preference measures derived from option prices and returns. The market for SPX options is one of the most active index option markets in the world. These options are European, have no wild card features, and can be hedged using the active market for S&P 500 index futures. We select the monthly quotes of options that are closest to 28 days from each month's expiration date and employ bid and ask prices. We also obtain the term structure of default-free interest rates from OptionMetrics. Following the procedure in Aït-Sahalia and Lo (2000) and other empirical studies on index options, we remove options that are illiquid and infer the option-implied underlying price to avoid nonsynchronous recording between options prices and the underlying index. More details on our sample of options data, their liquidity, and the related filtering procedures are provided in the Internet Appendix. We also obtain S&P 500 index returns to estimate the probability distribution function under the physical measure.
For analyses concerning mutual fund investments at the aggregate level, we directly employ aggregate monthly flows into active versus passive funds by investment categories as reported in Morningstar. We normalize flows into active and passive funds by the sum of their total net assets (TNAs). Since complete aggregate time series of data are not available for small- and medium-cap funds in Morningstar, especially in the early part of our sample period, our analyses mainly focus on large-cap funds to bootstrap fund returns and analyze the behavior of aggregate investments in actively managed funds, although we also check our main results using fund flows into the small- and medium-cap categories. In addition, we only examine large-cap growth and value funds in our analyses as blend funds tend to resemble both growth and value funds, making it difficult to identify the exact performance features that influence investors' decisions on individual categories of funds.
For analyses involving information aggregated from individual fund-level data, we extract our sample funds from the Center for Research in Security Prices (CRSP) Survivorship Bias Free Mutual Fund data set and Thomson Reuters mutual fund holdings data set for the period 1985 to 2008. To avoid outliers, we only keep funds with TNA exceeding $5 million. We then merge the CRSP data with the Morningstar fund categories to classify individual funds into the growth versus value investment categories. For funds that cannot be matched to Morningstar or have missing Morningstar investment categories, we identify their investment categories using the Lipper fund objective from the CRSP mutual fund data.
In TableI, we report summary statistics for our sample of actively managed funds. The median fund size as measured by TNA is relatively uniform across both LG and LV categories, but considerable cross-sectional variations are present in fund size both within and across categories. In particular, the mean fund size and fund flows are markedly larger than the median values, suggesting that some funds rake in significantly more money than the average fund. The returns of growth funds exhibit greater volatility than those of value funds. Finally, all of our sample active funds have relatively high levels of active management, as suggested by their high mean and medianActive Share(Cremers and Petajisto (2009)) of over 70%.
article.II. The Upside and Downside Performance:
Do actively managed funds offer different upside and downside performance features relative to passively managed funds? Further, how do these features vary across active funds' investment styles? We address these questions by comparing the distributional features of active funds' performance to those of several passive benchmarks.
We use bootstrapped fund returns to demonstrate three aspects of differences in return distributions between active and passive benchmarks. First, we consider unconditional return moments. We use volatility and skewness to measure the upside potential of active funds, and co-skewness with the market return and market beta to measure downside hedging. Second, to isolate the active component of fund returns, we construct market-neutral excess returns of active funds over their passive benchmarks. We use the skewness of excess returns to measure the incremental upside potential of active funds, and we use the conditional downmarket beta of excess returns to measure the downside-hedging ability of active funds. We also use the skewness of residuals from the conditional downmarket beta regression to evaluate active funds' upside potential properties irrespective of market conditions. Third, we investigate active funds' downside-hedging ability by estimating their excess return exposures to the aggregate jump and volatility risk factors. We start by describing our bootstrapping methodology and benchmark construction.
When examining the returns of the representative active fund, we do not focus on the average return across all active funds because the aggregate portfolio of all active funds resembles the passive market portfolio (Sharpe (1991)). We cannot use the return history of individual funds for moments estimation because funds differ in their starting and ending dates and because there may be gaps in reported monthly returns. In addition, the number of funds has increased considerably over our sample period as the industry has expanded. We therefore follow a bootstrapping procedure whereby we randomly pick one active fund from our sample of active funds, and hold it for a period of time that we refer to as the resampling period. At the end of the resampling period, a new fund is randomly drawn from our sample of funds and it is again held for the same resampling period, and so on. This strategy generates a path of monthly fund returns that we use to evaluate the return characteristics of active funds.
We use resampling periods of 1, 6, 12, or 36 months and find that our results are robust to the choice of resampling period. We therefore report the results only for six-month resampling period to save space. Furthermore, we account for differences in fund size using individual funds' prior-month TNA as the weight in the random draw at the beginning of the resampling period (equal-weighted results are similar and omitted for brevity). We conduct this bootstrapping procedure separately for the value and growth fund categories. To employ a time series as long as possible to increase the power of moments estimation, we draw individual funds' monthly returns starting from 1985 for the bootstrap analyses.
The number of paths affects the distribution of the sample statistics. We therefore choose the number of paths to be comparable to the average number of active funds (large-cap growth or value funds) during our sample period and draw multiple times to obtain the confidence interval of the sample means. Specifically, we draw 400 samples with 100 paths each. We construct statistics for each path and report the averages across all simulated paths. We also construct confidence boundswithineach sample of 100 funds to avoid artificial reduction of the standard errors due to a large simulation size and we report the averages across 400 samples.
For each active fund, we compute fund returns in two ways: the reported monthly net-of-fee returns and the gross monthly holdings-weighted returns of its equity positions. The former measure represents returns accessible to investors while the latter reflects returns earned by managers' equity investments. We employ the latter measure because we want to analyze performance differences between active and passive funds that are due to active management rather than other factors such as fees or nonequity holdings. For example, funds' holdings of cash may be timed to accommodate redemptions or increase with inflows that are functions of past performance. Focusing on the equity portion can also reduce the bias introduced by bootstrapping across funds with different levels of leverage. For example, if two funds hold the same equity index portfolio but a different (yet constant) percentage of cash, we would have a simulated path very different from the index using reported fund returns from these two funds. The simulated path would be exactly the same as that of the index, however, if we only compute the returns of the equity portion of the fund portfolio.
We employ three alternative passive benchmarks. First, we use the CRSP value-weighted market index returns as the benchmark because investing in the market portfolio is the simplest passive investment accessible to individual investors. Second, to account for the possibility that investors may engage in passive investments with a particular investment style, we form hypothetical passive portfolios that match the diversification level and holdings characteristics of the active funds under evaluation. Specifically, for each actively managed fund in each quarter, we replace each of its holdings with a randomly drawn stock with the size, book-to-market, and momentum quintile ranks in the prior quarter.9We then use the active fund's actual portfolio weights to compute the monthly return of its corresponding hypothetical benchmark portfolio during the quarter. We refer to this method as SBM or characteristic-based benchmarking.
We construct our third benchmark following Berk and van Binsbergen (2016) by projecting active fund returns onto the three Fama-French factors (market, book-to-market factor (HML), and size factor (SMB)) plus the momentum factor (MOM). The active fund excess return over the benchmark is the residual from the projection regression. As Berk and van Binsbergen (2016) argue, this factor-based benchmark may be hard to replicate for passive investors, and it may represent a high bar for detecting fund manager skill. It is nevertheless interesting to see if active funds deliver upside potential or downside protection with respect to such a strong benchmark.
In TableII, we present summary statistics on the returns of active funds and their passive benchmarks as well as the 95% confidence bounds for their differences. Results corresponding to the gross holdings-based returns are reported in the left panel while those corresponding to the net returns are reported in the right panel. Several notable patterns can be seen across the bootstrapped distributions of active and passive funds. First, not surprisingly, active funds exhibit roughly the same mean returns as the passive benchmarks before fees and slightly less after fees. Active LG funds' return volatility tends to be higher relative to all of their benchmarks although this effect is muted for net returns. For active LV funds, return volatility is slightly lower than the SBM-matched benchmark but a bit higher relative to the factor-based benchmark.
However, the most substantial differences are in skewness and co-skewness with the market index returns. Skewness of active funds is much less negative compared to that of the benchmarks. Active LV funds have skewness of −0.82 and −0.91 for gross holdings-based and reported net returns, respectively. This represents an increase in skewness relative to a benchmark with the increments ranging from 0.02 to 0.34 depending on the benchmark and active fund return employed. Compared with LV funds, active LG funds have considerably higher skewness of −0.61 and −0.65 for gross holdings-based and reported net returns, respectively. This represents an increase in skewness relative to a passive benchmark with increments ranging from 0.03 to 0.65.
The standard measure of market risk, beta with respect to a market index, is lower for active LV funds compared with their benchmarks, except in the case of the four-factor model–based benchmark where it is matched by construction. In contrast, active LG funds tend to have higher market betas relative to their benchmarks except in the case of the four-factor model benchmark and the SBM benchmark for net reported returns. Despite these differences in market risk, active funds appear to be able to increase skewness relative to their benchmarks and to reduce co-skewness risk with the market portfolio. Higher skewness may indicate larger upside potential in active funds while higher market co-skewness may indicate better downside hedging. Overall, active funds appear to provide greater upside potential and downside protection, relative to their passive benchmarks. We now analyze in more detail the differences in return properties across active LG and LV fund styles.
To isolate the active management component of a fund's returns and to better understand the marginal gain to an investor from switching from a passive to an active fund, we consider the properties of the excess returns of active funds over their passive benchmarks. We construct market-neutral excess returns of active funds over the benchmarks to eliminate any residual differences in their market beta. In the case of the four-factor model benchmark, the excess return is already market-neutral by design. For other benchmarks, we construct market-neutral excess returns by adjusting benchmark returns using the ratio of active fund beta to benchmark beta. We note, however, that all our results and conclusions based on excess returns hold if we simply compute the difference between active and passive returns.
To measure the upside potential resulting from active management, we compute the skewness of excess returns. Since excess returns are market-neutral by construction, the excess return skewness is idiosyncratic (fund-specific) skewness and indicates upside potential net of the benchmark.10We further examine total skewness by computing skewness of the residual term from regression1. If theβDof active funds' excess returns are negative, the higher skewness of active funds may come from hedging against downside risk through market timing and from upside seeking unconditional on market movements. The skewness of excess return residualecan thus help shed light on whether active funds boost skewness beyond market timing.
In TableIII, we report properties of excess returns over alternative benchmarks. We report averages across simulations for excess return skewness, residual skewness, β andβDfrom1. The “LB/UB” rows report, respectively, 95% confidence interval lower and upper bounds for each variable based on samples of 100 funds and averaged across 400 samples.
TheβDof active funds' excess returns is negative, regardless of the benchmark considered. It tends to be more negative for gross holdings-based returns, suggesting that cash holdings included in net reported returns tend to dampen variation in the conditional betas of the equity portfolios. The confidence bounds forβDwithin each sample also tend to be negative, indicating that the tendency to reduce beta in downturns is widespread among active funds. Overall, this evidence indicates that active funds reduce portfolio exposures to market risk during market downturns. Our results based on bootstrapping simulations are consistent with prior evidence on mutual funds' ability to hedge against market downturns (see, e.g., Glode (2011), Kacperczyk, Van Nieuwerburgh, and Veldkamp (2014), Bodnaruk, Chokaev, and Simonov (2016)).
TableIIIalso suggests that excess return skewness is positive for active LG funds, which is consistent with our prior finding that these funds have high skewness relative to the benchmarks. Moreover, even after we account for downside hedging, there remains considerable upside potential as measured by the skewness of the residual in regression1. The excess return skewness of LV funds is negative, but it is less negative than the benchmark skewness. This skewness boost of LV funds appears to come largely from hedging the downside risk because after accounting for variation in market beta, LV funds' residual returns have substantially negative skewness for all benchmarks considered.
The analysis on the downside beta of active funds' excess returns indicates that active funds can help reduce market risk during adverse market conditions. We now explore whether active funds' hedging property is related to jump and volatility risk. Both of these risks featured prominently in the asset pricing literature seeking to understand different sources of asset risk premiums. CHW show that jump and volatility risks represent economically significant risk factors distinct from those typically considered in linear factor models. They construct the jump and volatility factors using portfolios of index option straddles. One portfolio, volatility factor (VOL), is constructed as a delta-neutral and gamma-neutral strategy with positive vega (volatility) exposure. The other portfolio, jump factor (JUMP), is constructed as a delta- and vega-neutral strategy with positive gamma exposure, that is, it is affected only by larger market movements through delta-hedging errors. CHW show that these portfolios earn substantial risk premiums: sorting on the jump (volatility) factor beta results in a short-long portfolio return with Fama-French three-factor alpha of −9.4% (−2.7%) a year. Thus, positive loadings on the factors indicate hedging against jump or volatility risk while negative loadings represent risk exposure.
We use the option-based factors from CHW to estimate the hedging property of active LV and LG funds. Since data on these factors start from 1988, we use their overlap with our bootstrapped fund returns sample from 1988 to 2008 to perform the analysis. We regress the excess returns of active funds on these two factors and report the results in TableIV. The table reports the factor loadings along with the 95% confidence bounds on coefficients constructed within each random batch of 100 funds, averaged across 400 simulations.
The results suggest that LV funds have considerable hedging ability with respect to jump risk. Their jump factor loadings range from 0.1 to 0.5 using gross holdings-based returns and 0.05 to 0.36 for reported net returns. All 95% confidence interval lower bounds on loadings are well above 0, indicating robust statistical significance. The exposure of LV funds to the volatility factor is mixed, depending on the benchmark, indicating limited exposure to volatility risk. In terms of economic importance of the loadings, the jump loadings of active LV funds stand out because, as shown in CHW, the absolute value of the jump risk premium exceeds that of the volatility risk premium by nearly a factor of four.
For active LG funds, jump risk hedging tends to be weaker on average and also less robust across different return measures as compared to LV funds. There is some evidence of volatility hedging by LG funds when using net reported returns, but it is not robust to using holdings-based returns where it lacks uniform significance across benchmarks.
Overall, using the CHW jump and volatility factors, we find that active LV funds appear to show consistent ability to hedge against jump risk while potentially being exposed to limited volatility risk. In contrast, LG funds show weaker evidence of hedging against jump or volatility risk.
In summary, the returns of active funds and their excess returns over the passive benchmarks demonstrate that active funds help investors hedge downside risk and seek upside potential, with the extent varying considerably across fund styles. Active LV funds have relatively lower overall market betas and reduce their betas further during market downturns. Furthermore, they fare well during sudden market declines as measured by their significant hedging ability against the aggregate jump risk. Relative to passive benchmarks, active LV funds achieve some skewness boost, but it is usually much smaller compared with active LG funds and comes largely from downside hedging. On balance, relative to passive benchmarks, active LV funds appear to provide greater downside hedging.
In contrast, active LG funds typically have considerably high excess return skewness. The residual skewness of active LG funds' excess returns is also relatively high, suggesting that returns of active LG portfolios contain substantial upside potential beyond market timing. Although active LG funds' downside betas and volatility factor loadings indicate some hedging ability, their overall market betas are higher and they appear less effective than active LV funds in hedging aggregate jump risk. On balance, active LG funds provide greater upside potential relative to passive benchmarks.
These properties of active fund returns go beyond what can be derived from following simple buy-and-hold passive strategies or those based on return-predictive stock characteristics. Given that our results also hold for equity-only portfolios without any derivatives, the observed differences in returns between active and passive funds are likely attributed to active management such as stock selection or other dynamic trading strategies. We conjecture that the downside-hedging and upside-seeking features of active funds may be attractive to investors who value such return properties. We now develop a framework to study the link between investor preferences for upside potential and downside protection and the demand for actively managed funds.
article.III. Demand for Active Funds from Invest:
The bootstrap simulation results suggest significant differences in return distributions across active and passive funds. We would like to investigate empirically if such features can potentially be attractive to investors seeking upside potential or downside protection. Before we conduct empirical analysis, we evaluate this connection in the context of a theoretical portfolio choice model.
To understand the effects of β, note that the weighting function in3can be represented as a compound functionG(P;α,β)=G(Pβ;α,1). If we setα=1, then we obtainG(Pβ;1,1)=Pβ, which is uniformly concave (β<1) or convex (β>1). The former implies risk aversion (overweighting of the left tail) while the latter implies upside seeking (overweighting in the right tail). The effects of β on the weighting function and its density are shown in the lower row of panels in Figure1forβ<1. As β becomes lower, the weighting becomes more concave and the left tail becomes more overweighted. Thus, the coefficient β in the compound representation can independently reinforce or weaken risk attitude in the left tail through its interaction with α. Lower β corresponds to a uniform increase in risk aversion, while lower α corresponds to stronger risk aversion on the left and simultaneously stronger risk seeking on the right.
We use specification3in conjunction with simulated distributions of active and passive fund returns to investigate comparative statics of the demand for actively managed funds with respect to the two structural parameters of the probability weighting function, α and β. We consider a static portfolio choice problem where an investor has a choice of three assets: a risk-free asset, a passive fund, and an active fund of the same investment category. To compute the RDEU value function, we use the bootstrapped gross holdings-based returns described in the previous section and reduce these returns by median management fees for passive and active funds 50 and 110 bps per year, respectively.
We restrict all portfolios to be in the unit interval to preclude short-selling or purchase on margin and set the risk-free rate at 2% per annum. As the passive benchmark, we use the three-way characteristics-matched (SBM-matched) benchmark with six-month resampling (qualitatively, the results are not sensitive to benchmark or resampling interval chosen). We assume constant relative risk aversion (CRRA) utility functionu(w)=w1−γ/(1−γ)and normalize initial wealth to one. We set the power parameter toγ=5to ensure that the total and relative portfolio shares of risky assets are in the interior of a unit interval for our range of parameters. Qualitatively, the comparative statics of portfolio choice with respect to Prelec parameters are not very sensitive to the choice of γ. We use the rangeα∈[0.6,1]based on experimental estimates from the decision science literature (Camerer and Ho (1994)). Prelec β is usually fixed at one in most experimental work, so we useβ∈[0.8.1]to cover the range (across different option maturities) of median values estimated from the PK in Polkovnichenko and Zhao (2013).
Using bootstrapped distributions of funds in each category, we compute the optimal portfolio weights for two holding periodsnof three and five years:θ*(n)=[θ1*,θ2*,θ3*]for the risk-free asset, the passive fund, and the active fund, respectively. We then compute the fraction of the portfolio's risky assets invested in the active fund:θf(n)=θ3*θ2*+θ3*. Figures2and3present the results for value and growth styles, respectively. Each figure has two panels corresponding to the two holding periods. For the value fund we plotθfas a function of β (for different values of α) because β controls overweighting of unfavorable events and thus the preference for downside protection. For the growth fund, we plotθfas a function of α (for different values of β) because α controls the preference for upside potential.
Figure2shows how allocation to the active value fund changes with β for three different values ofα={1,0.8,0.7}. Lower values of β increase overweighting of unfavorable outcomes. Thus, for lower values of β, the concern for downside protection increases and hence so does the allocation to the active value fund. This allocation is similar whether we use a horizon of three or five years to compound simulated returns, but is more sensitive to β for lower values of α. This is because a lower α already generates overweighting of both tails of the distribution and the effect of β is to magnify the left tail further. Note that a lower α also leads to higher relative demand for the active value fund, which suggests that the combination of upside potential and downside protection is attractive to investors. Quantitatively, the effect from varying α is quite large and exceeds that of β. This is a property of the Prelec function that α affects overweighting in both tails and it can be shown that it has a larger effect than β on the overweighting of outcomes in the far left tail.12Because both parameters control overweighting in the left tail while only α controls the upside preference, the relative importance of these features for different types of distributions remains unclear and thus is an empirical question. Nonetheless, we can conjecture that downside risk aversion, rather than upside potential seeking, is the relatively more important driver of allocations to the active value fund because a lower α also leads to a smaller total allocation to the risky portion of the portfolio (not shown). To isolate the effects in the different parts of the return distribution in the empirical flow analysis, we orthogonalize α to β.
In Figure3, we show the allocation to the active growth fund as a function of α for three different values ofβ={1,0.9,0.8}. A lower α generates higher overweighting of the upside and increased relative demand for the active growth fund. A lower β implies slightly higher relative demand for the active growth fund, consistent with some hedging ability of active growth funds although this effect is small overall. For the growth fund, variation in β has a stronger effect on the total allocation to risky assets than on their composition.
Using the portfolio choice model from the previous section, we can find the implied fee that an investor would be willing to pay for access to the active portfolio. To do so, we use the gross returns from the same simulations used for portfolio allocation, that is, the same returns but without subtracting fees from either the passive or the active components. For each preference parameter combination, we compute two optimal portfolios: one with passive and active investment and one with passive investment only. The risk-free asset is always available. We convert each pair of value functions (with and without access to the active fund) into annualized certainty-equivalent (CE) returns. The difference in these CE returns corresponds to the annual fee out of total invested wealth an investor would be willing to pay to access the active portfolio. Dividing this difference by the fraction of the portfolio invested in the active fund, if it is nonzero, gives the percentage fee an investor would be willing to pay annually on the wealth invested in the active portion.
The passive benchmark available influences how much an investor values access to the active fund. We present the possible range of the fee values by considering two benchmarks: a sophisticated SBM-matched benchmark that dynamically matches the stock characteristics of the holdings of the active fund, and a basic benchmark of the market index. Figure4shows the results for the active value fund in the top row and the active growth fund in the bottom row. The left column shows results for the logarithmic investor and the right column for risk aversion ofγ=5. As before, we vary α in the active growth panels and β in the active value panels.13
Consider the top row of Figure4, which gives the results for value funds. When a log-investor (γ=1) has no additional overweighting of downside risk (β=1), she would pay a little over 20 bps per year to invest in the active value portfolio if she had access to the SBM-matched benchmark. The fee rises to over 30 bps for lower values of β. If the passive investment is holding the market index, the value of access to the active value fund is much higher and exceeds 100 bps (1%) for the range of β we consider. When investor risk aversion is higher,γ=5in the top right panel, the implied fee is over 50 bps for the SBM-matched benchmark and is about 90 bps for the market index. Thus, active value funds are more attractive to investors when they are more risk-averse in a conventional sense or overweight bad outcomes more heavily in the weighting function. The market index portfolio is less valuable as a passive option than the SBM-matched benchmark for the value category. While the latter result seems intuitive, it depends on active fund style and on investor risk aversion, as the next cases demonstrate.
Consider now the bottom row of Figure4, which gives the results for growth funds. A log-investor would pay about 30 to 35 bps to access the active growth portfolio when the passive alternative is the SBM-matched benchmark portfolio, and between 30 and 70 bps when it is the market index portfolio, depending on the value of α. Access to the growth fund is more valuable for lower α when the passive investment is the market index and is relatively flat when it is the SBM-matched benchmark. As we have already seen in the case of the value fund, the market index presents a less valuable passive option than the sophisticated SBM-matched benchmark (i.e., the value of access to the active fund is higher when compared to the market index portfolio as a passive alternative). This result changes when the investor is more risk-averse as shown in the bottom right panel forγ=5. An investor who does not overweight the upside significantly, that is, withα∈(0.851], would not invest in the active growth fund at all as indicated by the zero implied fee. Only if α is sufficiently low (i.e., strong upside potential preferences) would the investor pay to access the active growth fund in addition to the market index portfolio. When the passive alternative is the SBM-matched benchmark, the implied fee is close to 40 bps. Market index returns are less volatile than those of the SBM-matched portfolio and our results suggest that investors with higher risk-aversion would be less likely to select active growth portfolios when other, safer, passive portfolios are available.
The implied fees from the CE returns of the optimal portfolios suggest that investors would find access to active portfolios reasonably valuable. During our sample period and among our sample of equity funds, the fees of active funds have a median of approximately 110 bps with an interquartile range of 90 to 140 bps, while those of passive funds have a median of 51 bps and an interquartile range of 27 to 84 bps per year. So the typicaldifferencein fees between passive and active funds is on the order of 60 bps per year, which is in the same range as the implied fees we find (recall that the implied fee is computed using gross returns). If we assume that investors have access to the SBM-matched benchmark, a fee of 60 bps per year would be somewhat higher than what they would be willing to pay for the active portfolios in our computations. However, such a dynamically managed benchmark portfolio is likely to also be more costly or less accessible to average mutual fund investors. In contrast, if the passive alternative is the market index portfolio, then the opportunity to invest in an active fund could be worth over 90 to 100 bps per year, above the typical wedge between active and passive fees. Overall, the implied fees suggest that some investors may find active portfolios attractive.
A caveat is in order about interpretation of the quantitative results on implied fees. The implied fees we compute would not be the predicted sustainable active management fees in equilibrium. The portfolio model used to compute them is deliberately simple and excludes many of the well-known sources of background risk that investors face in real life. The computation of the CE returns that we rely on depends greatly on the particular menu of available risky assets and on the background risks investors face from other sources such as human capital, housing, small businesses, etc. While we believe that our results provide reasonable estimates of the value of active portfolios in a simple investment situation forsome investors, they should be interpreted with caution and an understanding of their limitations.
Before we embark on empirical analyses of the effect of investor preferences on fund flows, we discuss several issues related to the model and its link to our empirical work. The model helps motivate our empirical analyses by providing a plausible micro-foundation for the demand for active funds. It demonstrates that the return distributions of active funds are sufficiently distinct from those of passive benchmarks to generate a response from investors with tail-sensitive preferences. In the empirical exercise, we assume that the link between preferences and demand for active funds at the individual level carries over to the aggregate level and that we can detect the relation between aggregate preferences and aggregate fund flows in the data. For example, if current fundamental shocks lead investors to increase demand for assets with high upside potential, then aggregate representative preferences would reflect stronger upside potential seeking. Our empirical exercise then tests the effect of preference measures on aggregate flows into active funds with high upside potential (i.e., the LG funds).
While a full-fledged model with time-varying aggregate preferences and endogenous changes in active fund flows is outside the scope of our paper, it is useful to discuss what kind of mechanisms could potentially generate time variation in aggregate preferences and the associated fund flows that we observe empirically. There could be at least two broad potential sources of time variation in preferences at the aggregate level: those stemming from aggregation of heterogeneous preferences, and those stemming from aggregation of heterogeneous expectations. These two channels are not mutually exclusive and could both play important roles in determining the aggregate demand for mutual funds.
Typically, the estimation of preferences from aggregated economic variables (not only those from options) is done with an understanding that in the process we obtain “representative” preferences. The estimated preferences reflect individual decisions of multiple agents in the economy, with the aggregate preferences being a complex function of individual actions. When the conditional distribution of the fundamental state variables driving expected returns changes, investors respond by adjusting their portfolios to reflect new risk premiums in equilibrium. If investor risk preferences are heterogeneous, then individual changes in portfolio allocations will be heterogeneous and the distribution of assets across investors with different preferences will also be time-varying. In general, this will translate into time-varying “representative” preferences in the economy even when individual preferences are time-invariant or change very slowly. Likewise, changes in investor subjective expectations may also influence our inferences at the aggregate level because the standard econometric methods use distributions inferred from the data and do not account for heterogeneity in subjective expectations of individual agents. Variation in individual expectations, possibly through differential learning due to heterogeneous ability or constraints, would then lead to heterogeneous portfolio allocation and time-varying inferences about the aggregate “representative” preferences. In addition, either mechanism can potentially generate time-varying investor participation in risky asset markets, which could also be reflected in the aggregate preferences.
Overall, while a more sophisticated model of aggregate fund flows may provide a more nuanced view of the link between individual preferences and the demand for active funds as well as additional testable predictions, our current empirical methodology has its own merits. The strength of our empirical analysis is that we directly examine the link between aggregate preferences and active fund flows by measuring both in the data. Thus, our empirical results do not hinge on the source of variation in the aggregate PK or on assumptions about investors' utility functions.
The model and the empirical analysis of active fund flows also suggest that individual portfolios may provide additional clues about investor preferences. In the data, the estimates from the PK, on average, imply overweighting of both tails (Polkovnichenko and Zhao (2013)). Since these estimates are obtained from the prices of options on the aggregate market portfolio, it is possible that there exists heterogeneity among investors' weighting functions. That is, some investors may only overweight the downside and some may only overweight the upside, yet others may overweight both tails. Such heterogeneity at the individual level may manifest in portfolio decisions with potential clientéle effects where individual portfolios would include only funds with styles designed to enhance one side of the performance distribution. On the other hand, if the aggregation is a result of the inverse-S-shaped probability weighting functions of different degrees, then individual portfolios would contain a mixture of different fund styles. Although experimental studies typically find the inverse-S-shaped decision weights (Camerer and Ho (1994), Wu and Gonzales (1996)), cross-sectional variation of mutual fund selection in individual portfolios could be an interesting topic for future research to shed more light on investor preferences.
Overall, our analysis of the portfolio choice problem suggests that demand for active funds could potentially stem from particular features of their return distributions that correspond to more upside potential or downside protection relative to their passive counterparts. To investigate this possibility empirically, we employ two different proxies for the preferences for downside protection and upside potential. One is based on parameter estimates of Prelec α and β obtained by fitting a parametric specification of the PK. The other is based on the PK slopes on the left and right sides of the return distribution, which do not require assumptions on preferences. The latter measure requires only standard no-arbitrage conditions and provides more general evidence supporting the link between investor preferences and the demand for active funds. The former measures help corroborate this link with specific preference attributes from a structural model.
Following Polkovnichenko and Zhao (2013), we estimate probability weighting function parameters using prices of S&P 500 index options. Below, we briefly describe the estimation methodology; the Internet Appendix provides additional background details. For further technical assumptions and derivations, we refer readers to the original source.
We use monthly data for options with 28 days to expiration to construct a time series of the estimated coefficients α and β.15Note that the entire range of available moneyness is used to estimate the empirical PK to obtain the Prelec parameters. The options used in the construction of the PK are quite liquid and have been used in numerous prior studies in the literature (the Internet Appendix provides more details). Since α and β are correlated, because they both reflect the attitude toward downside risk, but only α reflects upside potential preferences, we orthogonalize α to β prior to using it in the time series regression so that the residual α mainly captures risk attitude toward the upside.
A word of caution is in order about interpretation of the results using Prelec function parameters for active fund flow regressions. These analyses should not be construed as a test of a specific model of the PK and are not designed to discriminate between alternative plausible formulations of the weighting functions that could fit the empirical PK. As discussed in Polkovnichenko and Zhao (2013), a sufficient property of the weighting function that generates U-shaped PK is its inverse-S shape. Several other functions considered in the literature also have an inverse-S shape and we are agnostic about which one may provide the best fit to the PK (see, e.g., Shefrin and Statman (2000) for several alternative specifications).16The purpose of the parametric measures is to establish an explicit link between the demand for active funds and structural parameters controlling specific features of preferences, and to corroborate the results based on the more generalized measures using the PK slopes defined in the next subsection.
A number of papers in the literature on option pricing present significant evidence that the PK may be nonmonotone (see, e.g., Aït-Sahalia and Lo (2000), Jackwerth (2000), Rosenberg and Engle (2002), Bakshi, Madan, and Panayotov (2010)), and in particular that it could be U-shaped. Bakshi, Madan, and Panayotov (2010) show that the nonmonotonicity of the PK is also consistent with negative expected returns on out-of-the-money (OTM) calls. Polkovnichenko and Zhao (2013) show that a nonmonotonic PK is consistent with preferences for upside potential in the return distribution.17
To constructQandP, we follow the estimation procedures developed originally in Aït-Sahalia and Duarte (2003) and Rosenberg and Engle (2002) and adopted for the estimation of the PK slopes in Polkovnichenko and Zhao (2013). We provide more detailed descriptions and additional references in the Internet Appendix.
Based on the properties of the active fund returns demonstrated earlier and the economic interpretations of the slopes and Prelec function parameters, we formulate two testable hypotheses for empirical analysis. First, a stronger preference for the upside (and higherSlope Upor lower Prelec α) is associated with stronger demand for active growth funds relative to passive growth funds. Second, stronger risk aversion (and higherSlope Downor lower Prelec β) corresponds to stronger demand for active value funds relative to passive value funds. Since flows effectively reveal variations in investor demand while the PK reveals variations in preferences, we can empirically test whether these relationships hold using data on active mutual fund flows and the PK slopes or Prelec parameters estimated from index options.
A word of caution is in order about interpreting the coefficients on upside preference in the flow regressions for value funds and those on downside preference for growth funds. While for robustness we include both upside and downside preferences in the flow regressions for each fund style, we do not attempt to predict the sign of the coefficients beyond the two hypotheses we propose above. That is, we do not expect that a stronger upside potential preference should be negatively related to flows into active value funds or that a stronger downside protection preference should be negatively related to flows into active growth funds. The empirical measures of preferences are not so highly correlated as to suggest that they substitute for each other, and our model cannot be used to make such auxiliary predictions. For example, when upside preferences become stronger, investors are expected to buy more active growth funds. However, this does not necessarily imply that investors will simultaneously sell active value funds to rebalance their portfolios. Although the model may appear to imply such a relationship, it is largely a product of the limited choices of assets in the model for tractability. In reality, investors may rebalance portfolios by reducing holdings of passive funds or safer assets such as bonds when the preference for upside seeking is stronger. While occasionally the coefficients on the auxiliary preference variables may imply rebalancing across active value and growth funds, these coefficients are not always reliable or statistically significant.
In our empirical analyses, we use the PK slopes constructed from options with 28 days to expiration and moneyness thresholds of 0.99 for the left side to capture the attitude toward downside risk and 1.01 for the right side to capture the attitude toward upside potential. We choose±1%moneyness for the slopes to maximize the use of available options data due to greater liquidity of options closer to the at-the-money (ATM) mark. However, in unreported robustness exercises, we also use 0.97 and 1.03 cutoffs for OTM slopes and obtain similar results. The slopes are tied to the points on a moneyness scale rather than to the cumulative probability because physical and risk-neutral distributions are time-varying, while constant moneyness allows us to construct comparable PK slopes across different months. Our estimation procedure for the PK uses the entire range of option strikes and therefore allows us to take advantage of the most liquid option contracts. Furthermore, to estimate the probability weighting function parameters as well as the slopes, we use the estimates of the probability distribution functions, which can be estimated more efficiently than the probability density functions (see the Internet Appendix for details). In the Internet Appendix, we reproduce summary statistics from Polkovnichenko and Zhao (2013) for the options used to construct the PK slopes and estimate the Prelec function parameters.
article.IV. Empirical Evidence from Fund Flows:
To test the effect of downside-hedging and upside-seeking preferences on the demand for active funds, we regress net flows into active funds on the contemporaneous option-implied slopes of the PK (SlopeUpandSlopeDown). We also use parameters estimated by fitting Prelec weighting function to the empirical PK as alternative preference measures that have structural theoretical interpretations. Because Prelec α affects behavior in both tails while β affects only the left tail, we orthogonalize α to β and use the residual α in the flow regressions. Our baseline analyses focus on large-cap value and growth funds, but we also present additional time series results for medium-cap and small-cap active funds. We employ the period 1996 to 2008 given the availability of S&P 500 index options data from OptionMetrics.
Prior studies show that flows into different fund categories are related to investor preferences for specific fund styles (Sirri and Tufano (1998)). We therefore conduct separate analyses for fund flows for different investment categories and control for flows to passive funds within the same category. We normalize flows into active funds and those into passive funds in each investment category by the sum of the TNAs of these two types of funds in the category. Controlling for flows into passive funds of the same investment objective can help capture flow variations that are due to factors affecting investor sentiment for a particular fund style or equity funds in general. In unreported analyses, we also reestimate all of our results using the difference between active and passive flows as the independent variable and find that the results are very similar.
In addition, we include the 12-month cumulative TNA-weighted monthly net-of-fee returns of each investment category as controls to account for flows resulting from the return-chasing behavior of mutual fund investors. Warther (1995) shows that aggregate flows into equity mutual funds are correlated with stock market returns. We therefore also control for contemporaneous market returns to capture the effect of macroeconomic factors on mutual fund investments. In some specifications, we replace cumulative category returns with cumulative market returns to account for a lag in investor reaction to market conditions.20We proxy for market returns using the returns of the CRSP value-weighted market index. Finally, we control for the monthly investor sentiment measure of Baker and Wurgler (2006,2007). This measure is a composite sentiment index based on the first principal component of a number of proxies for sentiment as suggested in prior literature.21Given the potential autocorrelation in fund flows, we reportt-statistics computed using Newey and West (1987) autocorrelation- and heteroskedasticity-consistent standard errors with a data-driven number of lags. All variables are standardized to have a mean of 0 and standard deviation of 1. The coefficient estimates from these time series regressions and theirt-statistics are presented in TableV.
TableVshows that flows into active funds are significantly positively correlated with past category or market returns, and with flows into passive funds with the same investment style. This is expected since flows into active funds should be influenced by their recent performance as well as investor preferences for certain fund styles in general.
For LV funds, we find a significantly positive loading of fund flows onSlopeDown. This result indicates that more money flows into active value funds when investors are more concerned about downside risk. This is consistent with our earlier observation from the empirical return moments that value funds reduce their betas during market downturns and reduce exposure to aggregate jump risk relative to their benchmarks. Also, the economic magnitude of the effect ofSlopeDownon flows into actively managed value funds is comparable to that of cumulative category returns.
For LG funds, we find a significantly positive loading of flows onSlopeUp. This is consistent with the properties of growth funds' return moments presented earlier. Recall that their excess returns over those of their passive benchmarks exhibit large positive skewness and residual skewness in the conditional beta regression. In terms of economic significance, a one-standard-deviation increase inSlopeUpwould lead to a sizable increase in monthly flows into actively managed growth funds, with the magnitude comparable to that of a one-standard-deviation increase in contemporaneous market returns or cumulative category returns.
The flow regressions show that when the demand for upside potential or downside protection implied in index option prices is stronger, fund flows are directed more toward active growth funds or value funds, respectively. The revealed preferences from flows suggest that active funds apparently can better cater to investor preferences as compared to passive funds with the same investment style. We next examine this result more directly by using structural preference parameters estimated by fitting the PK to the parametric specifications based on the Prelec probability weighting function. TableVIshows the results of replacing the PK slopes with estimates of Prelec α and β. Note that α is orthogonalized to β and thatlowervalues of these parameters correspond togreaterupside potential and downside protection preferences, respectively. As a result, the expected sign of the estimated Prelec α and β will be opposite to that of the estimated slope coefficients.
Consider specifications (1) and (2) for LV funds. Flows into LV funds have significantly negative sensitivity to Prelec β parameter, implying that flows to active value funds increase as investors become more concerned with downside risk. This is consistent with the structural interpretation of Prelec β and with the results presented earlier based on the downside slope of the PK. The economic significance of the effect is again quite large and exceeds that of contemporaneous market returns or cumulative category returns.
For LG funds in specifications (5) and (6), we find that flows into active growth funds load significantly negative on Prelec α. Thus, as α becomes lower and investor preferences for upside potential increase, so do the flows to active growth funds. This confirms our earlier result based on theSlopeUpcoefficient. The economic magnitude of the Prelec α effect on flows is also comparable to that of contemporaneous market returns or cumulative category returns.
To summarize, flows into active growth and value funds show investors' revealed preferences for upside potential or downside protection, respectively. The results are qualitatively the same regardless of whether we use slopes of the PK or fitted structural parameters to examine the relation between investor preferences and fund flows. They are also consistent across specifications that control for cumulative lagged market versus category returns. Additional robustness tests that control for other potential factors influencing fund flows are reported in the Internet Appendix.
The flow-preference relationship we identify has interesting implications for the debate in the literature on measures of managerial skill. Berk and Green (2004) and Berk and van Binsbergen (2015), among others, interpret the evidence that flows respond to past performance as consistent with the presence of managerial skill. Our results suggest that active fund flows are linked with measures of investor risk preferences for performance in the tails of the return distribution. Such features of investor preferences are typically outside of the standard EU model and require alternative mechanisms to generate preferences for upside potential and downside protection.22Thus, if flows rationally respond to skill and reveal investor preferences, the value of active management may also stem from tail properties of the return distribution of active funds.
The PK used to construct slopes and preference parameters may be related to existing measures of investor sentiment because they are constructed from market-wide indicators. To control for this possibility, we include the Baker-Wurgler (2006) sentiment index in base-case specification throughout the paper, although we do not find that it significantly affects flows beyond other included variables. Here, we investigate whether our results are sensitive to controlling for market conditions using two alternative measures. First, we include the NBER recession indicator since investor sentiment is usually closely related to overall economic conditions. Recessions are likely to be associated with low investor sentiment. Second, we use the VIX as a proxy for investor sentiment. The VIX may contain information on investor preferences because, like our measures, it is constructed based on information from index options.
TablesVandVIpresent estimates from the regressions of net flows into active funds on option-implied measures of investor risk attitude, controlling for the additional measures of investor sentiment. Specifications (3) and (4) present the results for value funds and specifications (7) and (8) present the results for growth funds. The results indicate that after controlling for investor sentiment, we still find a significantly positive loading of fund flows onSlopeDown(negative loadings on Prelec β) for value funds and a significantly positive loading onSlopeUp(negative loadings on Prelec α) for growth funds. That is, when investors have a stronger preference for downside protection, we observe greater flows into actively managed value funds. On the other hand, when investors have greater preference for upside potential, we observe greater flows into actively managed growth funds. In contrast, the NBER recession indicator and VIX do not appear to have additional explanatory power for monthly fund flows after we control for the effect of investor risk preferences.
Note that the results of controlling for these proxies of market sentiment are somewhat expected. One important distinction between our risk preference measures and prior documented investor sentiment or market-wide implied volatility measures is that our slope measures separately identify preferences for downside protection versus upside potential. When a sentiment index is high, it could be because investors are less concerned about downside risk, are more excited about upside potential, or both. Our measures, in contrast, can differentiate the demand for individual fund investments featuring payoffs in different parts of the return distribution. In addition, while VIX is also derived from index option prices, it does not incorporate information from the underlying physical distribution of the index return. In unreported analyses, we find that these sentiment measures commonly used in the literature are not highly correlated with the slopes on either side or with the Prelec (1998) parameters, suggesting different information content of these variables.23
Our analyses of the relationship between flows into active funds and preference measures thus far focus on large-cap funds. For completeness, we now present results of our baseline analysis for medium- and small-cap funds. We note that the number of observations used in the regressions is significantly smaller for some fund groups due to the shorter time series of aggregate flows into passive funds as provided in Morningstar.
TableVIIshows that, for active medium- and small-cap value funds, flows are positively related toSlopeDownand negatively related to Prelec β. These results generally maintain similar economic and statistical significance to those observed from large-cap funds in TablesVandVI.
In contrast, flows into both medium- and small-cap growth funds have positive loadings onSlopeUpand negative loadings on Prelec α. The sign and magnitude of the coefficients are consistent with the estimates obtained for large-cap funds, while the statistical significance is lower. This may be due to the shorter sample period and the fact that the upside preference proxies we estimate are based on index options of large-cap stocks.
