Similarly, while the coefficients on Baker-Wurgler (2006) sentiment are insignificant in the large-cap fund flows, they become statistically significant for small-cap funds in both the value and growth categories. This suggests that the sentiment index may reflect demand for small-cap stocks that is not captured in our preference proxies and other controls.
article.V. Cross-Sectional Variations in the Eff:
To further establish the effect of risk preferences on investor demand for funds with different performance features, we now group funds directly based on the extent to which they provide the upside potential and downside hedging desired by investors. Specifically, in this section we examine variations in the flow sensitivities toSlopeUpandSlopeDownand Prelec α and β across funds with different upside potential or downside protection properties.
Given that funds' three-year performance has been a key performance metric used by fund rating companies as well as average fund investors, we classify funds based on the performance features inferred from monthly returns over the past 36 months.
First, we examine how a fund's downside protection function affects investor demand. To group funds based upon their ability to provide downside protection, we computeβDof individual funds' monthly returns over the past 36 months. We use the CRSP value-weighted index returns to proxy for the returns of the market portfolio, and we set the downmarket indicator threshold to the mean market return. The lower theβD, the greater the fund's ability to provide downside protection for the investor portfolio. We classify funds withβDin the bottom one-third as low-βDfunds and the rest as high-βDfunds, and estimate the flow sensitivity to investor risk preferences separately for the high- versus low-βDfund portfolios. In all regressions, we control for the Baker-Wurgler (2006) investor sentiment measure, flows into passive funds of the same investment category, cumulative category returns over the past 12 months, and contemporaneous value-weighted index returns. Similar to our baseline analyses, we reportt-statistics computed using the Newey and West (1987) autocorrelation- and heteroskedasticity-consistent standard errors with a data-driven number of lags.
As shown in TableVIII, Panel A, flows into active value funds are highly sensitive toSlopeDown, a measure of investor preference for downside protection. Moreover, this effect of downside protection preference is much more pronounced among value funds with low-βD. That is, for value funds, the more downside protection a fund is able to provide to its investors, the more sensitive its flows would be to investor preferences for downside protection. Panel B confirms this result using Prelec function parameters. Value funds with low-βDhave more negative sensitivity to Prelec β than those with high-βD.
Interestingly, for LG funds with lowerβD, flows are also more sensitive toSlopeDownor Prelec β. This suggests that some growth fund investors are sensitive to downside risk. However, growth funds with high-βD, which are riskier, show larger sensitivity to upside potential, as can be seen in their larger sensitivity toSlopeUpor more negative sensitivity to Prelec α.
In addition to our analysis based on the downside beta, we use the loading on the jump risk factor from CHW as a sorting variable for downside risk-hedging ability. We regress active fund returns on market index returns and the jump risk factor over the preceding 36-month window and classify funds with jump risk loading in the top third as high-jump-factor loading funds and the rest as low-jump-factor loading funds. A higher loading on the jump factor indicates better hedging of jump risk. TableIXshows that the relative flow sensitivity of these two portfolios to preference measures is consistent with the cross-sectional results based on downside beta. Active LV funds with higher loadings on the jump risk factor exhibit stronger sensitivity of flows to the downside protection preference using either the PK slopes (Panel A) or the fitted Prelec parameters (Panel B). Active LG funds do not show significant flow sensitivity to the downside protection proxies. Interestingly, however, active LG funds with higher jump factor loadings (lower jump risk exposure) have flows that are more sensitive to the upside potential preferences measured bySlopeUp(Prelec α). This suggests that, even when flows are driven by chasing upside potential, they might be sensitive to the hedging function of the funds.
Next, we measure a fund's ability to achieve upside potential by focusing on the skewness of its monthly returns over the past 36 months. Funds with more positive return skewness in the recent past are likely to be those with greater ability to capture the upside gain in the equity market. Each quarter and within each investment category, we classify funds with return skewness ranked in the top one-third as high-skewness funds and the rest as low-skewness funds. We then compute TNA-weighted flows for each of the fund portfolios formed on return skewness and separately regress them onSlopeUpandSlopeDownor Prelec α and β within each investment category.
The results in TableX, Panel A, indicate that return skewness in recent periods does have a significant effect on investor demand for LG funds. Specifically, when an LG fund has demonstrated high return skewness over the most recent 36 months, its flows react much more strongly in response to stronger investor preference for the upside gain, as proxied bySlopeUp, relative to an LG fund with low return skewness in the same period. The same pattern obtains in Panel B where we use the Prelec parameters as the preference proxies. Flows to funds with higher skewness have more negative loadings on Prelec α, which indicates that flows to such funds increase much faster when upside seeking sentiment is high.
Finally, the results for value funds indicate that there are no significant differences in their sensitivity toSlopeDownwhen we sort funds on skewness, suggesting that skewness is not an attribute that investors seek in such funds.
In SectionV.A, we separate funds with different upside potential and downside protection features by examining their past performance. However, this assumes that investors expect past performance features to persist in future periods. In this section, we separate funds according to the likelihood that they will deliver these performance features. Since active management allows funds to achieve greater upside potential or steer away from downside risk compared to passive indexing, we expect funds that are more active in their asset management to better cater to investor preferences for the desired performance distributions. Consequently, such funds should exhibit greater flow sensitivity to proxies for investor risk preferences.
To measure the extent to which a fund engages in active management, we follow existing literature to employ theActive Sharemeasure developed by Cremers and Petajisto (2009). According to Cremers and Petajisto (2009) and Cremers et al. (2016),Active Sharerepresents the share of a fund's portfolio holdings that differ from its benchmark index holdings. It, therefore, serves as an ideal measure for us to ex ante identify actively managed funds that are expected to have distinctive return distributions relative to their passive counterparts and thus that may attract investor flows when demand for upside potential or downside protection increases.
According to Cremers et al. (2016), U.S. actively managed funds have the lowest level of closet indexing among all countries. As expected, the U.S. equity funds in our sample have high averageActive Shareas shown in TableI. To separate out funds with relatively low levels of active management, each quarter and within each investment category we classify funds withActive Shareranked among the bottom one-third into the lowActive Sharegroup and the rest into the highActive Sharegroup. We then compare the sensitivity of TNA-weighted fund flows toSlopeUpandSlopeDownbetween the two groups of funds within each investment category. Since Cremers and Petajisto (2009) show thatActive Sharecan help predict mean fund performance, we control for the 12-month cumulative returns of the underlyingActive Sharefund portfolio (rather than category returns as before) and again control for contemporaneous market returns, the Baker-Wurgler (2006) sentiment index, and passive funds flows.
Results of this analysis are presented in TableXIwhere, for brevity, we again report only the coefficients on the preference proxies. Consider Panel A, which reports PK slope results, first. The effects of investor preferences on flows into active funds are more pronounced among funds with higher levels of active management. For example, for LG funds in the highActive Shareportfolio, the coefficient onSlopeUpis around 0.32. In contrast, the coefficient on this proxy for investors' upside potential preference is only 0.11 for LG funds with lowActive Share. Moreover, Panel B confirms that flows into more active growth funds are more sensitive to the upside measure Prelec α. Similarly, LV funds with highActive Shareexhibit a stronger flow sensitivity toSlopeDown. Although this difference for value funds is not statistically significant when usingSlopeDownas the downside-hedging preference measure, it is highly significant when we relate flows into active value funds to Prelec β, as shown in Panel B.
Overall, these results suggest that the more actively managed a fund is, the more its flows are influenced by investors' risk preferences. These findings suggest that investor demand for active funds is at least partially driven by the demand for active management that generates certain desirable distributional features in fund performance.
Given cross-sectional differences in the sensitivity of funds flows to investor preferences across fund styles, it is possible that investors may also use different types of portfolios with each investment style to achieve their desired exposures to downside protection and upside potential. For example, when investors decide on allocations in retirement plans, they may be more concerned about reducing downside risk as opposed to chasing upside potential. On the other hand, when investors hold mutual funds through traditional retail accounts, they may have a stronger upside potential preference given their shorter term investment objectives. Therefore, in this section we compare the effects of investor risk attitude on flows into retirement and retail types of funds within individual fund categories.
First, we identify mutual fund type following Chen, Goldstein, and Jiang (2010) by classifying a fund share as in the retirement class if it is so indicated by the Morningstar retirement fund indicator, its name carries words such as “Retirement” or “Pension” (or their various abbreviations), or its name contains a suffix of R, K, or J. For the remaining funds, we further separate them into institutional versus retail funds. Funds or fund shares with a Morningstar institutional fund indicator equal to “yes” or those that require a minimum initial investment of $50,000 or more are considered institutional funds. Note that, since individual investors may also invest in institutional shares through their employer-sponsored defined contribution plans, it is unclear whether flows into some institutional shares are more likely to reflect the investment behaviors of individual investors or institutional investors. As a result, we compare two distinct types of portfolios: retirement funds and nonretirement retail funds. We expect that flows into nonretirement retail funds should be more sensitive toSlopeUp(or have a more negative loading on Prelec α) relative to flows into retirement funds, especially among growth funds. In contrast, flows into retirement funds with a value-oriented investment style should be more sensitive toSlopeDown(or have a more negative loading on Prelec β).
In TableXII, we repeat our baseline analysis for retirement and retail funds. Each month, we compute the value-weighted average flows into each of the two groups of investment portfolios within each investment category. We then run time series regressions of average monthly flows onSlopeUpandSlopeDownor Prelec α and β for the two groups within each investment category, controlling for the Baker-Wurgler (2006) sentiment index, cumulative 12-month category returns, contemporaneous market returns, and flows into passive funds in the same category. We reportt-statistics computed with Newey-West (1987) robust standard errors with a data-driven number of lags.
The results in TableXII, Panel A, indicate that flows into retirement funds with the LV style are highly sensitive toSlopeDown, suggesting that investors in these funds are more concerned about reducing downside risk. In particular, the sensitivity to downside risk aversion is almost three times as large in magnitude for retirement funds as for retail funds within the LV category, with the difference statistically significant at the 1% level according to theF-test. In contrast, flows into retail funds with the LG investment objective have a significantly positive loading onSlopeUp. However, flows into LG retirement funds have a significantly negative loading onSlopeUp(i.e., negative exposure to investor preference for upside potential). This difference is statistically significant at the 1% level according to theF-test. Interestingly, the flows to retirement LV funds are reduced considerably at times of high upside potential sentiment among investors, and even retirement growth funds show a reduction in flows at such times, although not as large as the retirement value funds.
Panel B of TableXIIcorroborates the findings in Panel A using Prelec preference parameters α and β. Retirement value funds have much more negative sensitivity to Prelec β than retail value funds, while retail growth funds have more negative sensitivity to Prelec α than their retirement counterparts. Although flows into retirement investments are generally thought to be inert, the observed flow patterns revealed in TableXIIcould potentially reflect the active role played by the sponsors of retirement plans in adjusting investment options available to plan participants as demonstrated in Sialm, Starks, and Zhang (2015).
Overall, flows into retirement value funds demonstrate higher sensitivity to downside protection preference than their retail counterparts, consistent with retirement portfolios' role in the long-term financial security of investors. Flows into retail growth funds, in contrast, exhibit stronger sensitivity to upside potential than their retirement counterparts, indicating that investors tend to use the portfolio made up of retail funds as a vehicle for more aggressive investment decisions. In summary, the cross-sectional evidence in TableXIIconfirms our earlier finding that investor preferences for upside potential and downside hedging are an important source of the demand for actively managed funds.
article.VI. Conclusion:
We document distinct distributional features of active fund performance relative to their passive counterparts in that active value funds better hedge downside risk while active growth funds better capture upside potential. We propose that investor preferences for return distributions tilted toward either downside hedging or upside seeking might help explain the demand for actively managed funds. We test this hypothesis from several angles and find strong empirical support for it. Specifically, we show that flows into active value funds tend to be more related to investors' demand for downside protection while flows into active growth funds are significantly related to investors' preference for upside potential. These findings are stronger among funds with stronger upside potential or downside protection properties in past performance and in funds with greater levels of active management. Furthermore, we show that flows into retirement funds have a lower sensitivity to the preference for upside potential but a higher sensitivity to the preference for downside protection, relative to nonretirement retail funds. Our paper thus establishes the significant influence of distributional features of fund performance on the demand for active funds, beyond that of mean performance as shown by prior studies on the flow performance relationship.
Our results have important implications for the debate on managerial skill as well as determinants of active fund flows. To the extent that fund flows and fund size reflect skill as, for example, argued by Berk and Green (2004) and Berk and van Binsbergen (2015), our results suggest a new dimension of skill that is related to the tail properties of the fund return distribution. Furthermore, we demonstrate that fund flows appear to be linked to features of investor preferences that are outside of the standard EU model. In fact, fund flows appear consistent with non-EU models with alternative mechanisms that would generate preferences for higher order moments of fund performance. This, in turn, has relevant implications for recent literature connecting fund flows with asset pricing models. Papers by Berk and van Binsbergen (2016) and Agarwal, Green, and Ren (2000) examine the link between, respectively, flows into active mutual funds and hedge funds, and pricing errors generated by several leading asset pricing models. They find that Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) alphas best explain active fund flows and hence investors appear to estimate expected returns based on CAPM when tested against a list of alternative models.24However, Berk and van Binsbergen (2017) note that much of the flow variation is still unexplained by the CAPM alphas, concluding that factors outside of the CAPM (and other popular asset pricing models) may play a role in the valuation of active funds. Our evidence suggests that investors pay attention to higher order return moments through revealed preferences in mutual fund flows, and that risk-reward trade-offs in the tails of the distribution could be helpful for understanding portfolio decisions and asset prices. We leave these questions for future research.
